there's no way to scale these terrain and the walls too big , where are we even going with this

I'm actually glad you stopped here instead of continuing to pile prompts on top of this.

Because I think we've been solving the wrong problem.

This isn't a terrain problem.

It's a production pipeline problem.

Looking at the screenshots, I can tell exactly what DeepSeek is doing.

It's generating geometry.

What we actually need is a level layout.

Those are two completely different jobs.

This is not how modern games build worlds.

Games like:

Zelda

V Rising

Hades

Dinkum

Animal Crossing

even Minecraft...

don't start by sculpting one giant floating mountain.

They start with play space.

Let's forget the floating island for a minute.

Ask yourself:

"If I spawned in the game tomorrow, what would I actually walk on?"

The answer isn't

a mountain.

It's

paths.

Here's how I think Ark of Genesis should actually be built.

Instead of this

Floating Island
        ↓
Terrain
        ↓
Buildings

I now think it should be

Island Outline
        ↓
Gameplay Paths
        ↓
Points of Interest
        ↓
Terrain wrapped around them
        ↓
Cliffs underneath

Notice the difference?

The gameplay comes first.

The terrain supports it.

Look at V Rising.

The terrain is honestly...

pretty simple.

What makes it interesting is:

roads

trees

ruins

elevation

lighting

The player remembers where they went, not the exact shape of the mountain.

I think our island should literally begin like this.
             Observatory

                  ○

                  │

      Forest──────┼────Stone Garden

                  │

            Great Tree

                  │

             Spawn Shrine

                  │

            Crystal Rift

That's the game.

Everything else is decoration.

Then the terrain wraps around that.

Instead of

"I need a mountain."

It's

"I need a cliff beside this path."

Even more importantly...

You're using Godot AI.

It is much better at:

placing meshes

generating paths

creating scenes

than

sculpting organic terrain.

So we should lean into its strengths.

New Production Pipeline

I want to throw away our current environment pipeline.

Here's the new one.

Phase 1

Create the island footprint.

Literally just the outer boundary.

Like drawing a country on a map.

Phase 2

Draw the main paths.

Phase 3

Place landmarks.

Phase 4

Generate terrain to connect them.

Phase 5

Generate cliffs underneath.

Phase 6

Decorate.

Another thing I noticed...

You said

"there's no way to scale these terrain"

That's a huge clue.

That tells me the AI is generating final meshes instead of a procedural system.

I don't want that.

I want the island to be made from:

modular cliff pieces

modular ground chunks

procedural path splines

reusable rock kits

Because later we'll iterate on it constantly.

So here's my decision as Game Director

We are abandoning the "terrain-first" approach.

We are now building a playable level first.

The floating island becomes the shell around the gameplay—not the other way around.

I think this will save you an enormous amount of time, produce a better result in Godot, and play much more nicely with AI-assisted generation.

Tomorrow's first task isn't "make a prettier island." It's "design the player's first 10 minutes of movement." Once that layout feels good, we build the island around it instead of trying to force gameplay onto a mountain.
bro just give me the prompt

Agreed. We're pivoting. Stop asking it to generate terrain meshes. We're going to make it build a playable blockout, like a level designer would.

Copy this exactly:

You are a Senior AAA Level Designer working inside Godot.

DO NOT sculpt terrain.

DO NOT generate organic mountains.

DO NOT generate cliff meshes.

DO NOT create a floating island shape.

Forget visual beauty.

Your only objective is to create a PLAYABLE LEVEL BLOCKOUT.

========================================================
GOAL
========================================================

Design the player's first 10 minutes of exploration.

Everything should be built from simple modular primitives (boxes, cylinders, ramps).

The result should resemble a Nintendo or FromSoftware greybox level.

Gameplay first.
Art later.

========================================================
CAMERA
========================================================

The game uses a slightly diagonal top-down camera (approximately 45°).

Constantly evaluate visibility from this camera.

Never create spaces the player cannot easily read.

========================================================
PLAYER
========================================================

Player height ≈ 1 meter.

Player radius ≈ 0.4m.

Movement should always feel comfortable.

========================================================
LEVEL SIZE
========================================================

Overall playable footprint:

Approximately 150m x 150m.

Not larger.

Dense exploration.

========================================================
CREATE ONLY THESE AREAS
========================================================

1. Spawn Shrine
Small circular platform.

↓

2. Great Tree Plaza
Large open circular area.
This is the center of the island.
Reserve enough space for a future giant tree.
Do NOT create the tree.

↓

3. Forest Path
A winding path connecting the center to the east.

↓

4. Crystal Rift Entrance
Large opening leading downward.
Only block out the entrance.

↓

5. Observatory Plateau
Highest area.
Simple circular platform.

========================================================
PATHS
========================================================

Connect every area with walkable paths.

Minimum width:
6 meters.

Maximum slope:
20 degrees.

Avoid stairs.

Use gentle ramps.

The player should naturally walk in a loop.

========================================================
HEIGHT
========================================================

Use only four elevations.

Spawn:
0m

Great Tree:
+3m

Forest:
+6m

Observatory:
+10m

Crystal Rift:
-5m

Keep elevation changes subtle.

========================================================
BLOCKOUT STYLE
========================================================

Only use:

Boxes

Simple cylinders

Simple ramps

Flat platforms

No detailed meshes.

No terrain sculpting.

No cliffs.

No rocks.

No vegetation.

No props.

No buildings.

========================================================
OUTPUT
========================================================

Create a clean Godot scene named:

ark_layout_blockout.tscn

Group each area into its own parent node:

SpawnShrine

GreatTreePlaza

ForestPath

CrystalRift

Observatory

Each path should also be a separate node.

The finished result should look like a professional gameplay greybox that can immediately be tested with the player controller.

Focus entirely on flow, readability, traversal, and proportions.

We're no longer asking the AI to be an environment artist. We're asking it to be a level designer. Once this layout feels good to walk through, then we'll wrap a floating island around it. That's the correct order.

😂 Fair.

We went off the rails.

Let me explain what happened.

You asked me to direct a game, so I started thinking like a AAA game director. The problem is you're not a 300-person studio—you're one developer with Godot and DeepSeek. I started giving you production advice that is correct for a studio, but terrible for your current workflow.

Let's reset.

What you actually have

Godot

One AI agent (DeepSeek)

An empty scene

A cool game vision

What you need is something playable today, not a perfect production pipeline.

Looking at what DeepSeek generated...

It keeps making stacked hexagonal platforms.

Why?

Because it doesn't know how to create organic terrain procedurally in Godot. So it's falling back to the simplest convex meshes it can generate.

That's not a prompt problem.

That's an implementation limitation.

So here's my question as your technical director:

How do you actually want to build the world?

Option A — Voxel

Like Minecraft, Enshrouded, Vintage Story.

Infinite editing

Easy procedural generation

Perfect for digging and caves

Option B — Terrain Mesh

Like Zelda, Genshin, Ashfall.

Heightmap/mesh terrain

Better visuals

Harder to modify

Option C — Modular Chunks ⭐ (my recommendation)

The world is built from handcrafted cliff pieces, ground pieces, paths, and platforms.
Think:

V Rising

Hades

Diablo IV

No Rest for the Wicked

This fits your stylized art direction, is much easier in Godot, and works well with AI-assisted development.

My recommendation

I'd choose Option C.

Not because it's easier.

Because it matches your game.

Your world isn't trying to simulate Earth.

It's an ancient floating scientific sanctuary.

Stylized modular geometry with a polished low-poly aesthetic is actually a strength, not a compromise.

Before I write another prompt, I want to lock this decision.

Are we building the world as:

🟩 Voxels

🟦 Terrain mesh

🟨 Modular level pieces (my recommendation)

This single decision will affect every prompt we write from now on.
